context.description: Chapter 5, "The Symbolic World," explains how symbolization abstracts specific situations into universal images and rules that can be reused across contexts. It uses the street-and-car example to show how people rely on simple heuristics instead of detailed physical reasoning to judge danger. The page argues that this internal symbolic world can feel more real than the external world.
normalization_notes: Translated visible headings into English for the normalized surface.
Chapter and section structure is explicit; page content is prose about symbolic cognition.

rows.paragraph_text: Symbolism is presented as abstraction of specific circumstances into universal images and rules that can be applied to other situations. The text argues that the mind builds an internal symbolic world that simplifies reality and can guide faster understanding.
rows.text_content: Symbolism is presented as abstraction of specific circumstances into universal images and rules that can be applied to other situations. The text argues that the mind builds an internal symbolic world that simplifies reality and can guide faster understanding.

rows.paragraph_text: A street-and-car example shows that people do not calculate speed, mass, friction, and reaction times in order to avoid danger. Instead, they apply a simple heuristic that treats a moving car as immediate danger and keeps distance.
rows.text_content: A street-and-car example shows that people do not calculate speed, mass, friction, and reaction times in order to avoid danger. Instead, they apply a simple heuristic that treats a moving car as immediate danger and keeps distance.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage emphasizes that symbolic models can be easier to communicate than detailed physical explanations and can feel more real than the external world.
rows.text_content: The passage emphasizes that symbolic models can be easier to communicate than detailed physical explanations and can feel more real than the external world.
